# dig — full reference for LLMs

> Open, local, reversible data + retrieval + memory primitive for AI agents. This file is a
> self-contained briefing: what dig is, how it's built, its CLI surface, its policy model,
> its retrieval system, and its measured results. For canonical detail, read the linked
> source docs — this summarizes them.

## 1. What dig is

A company's or a person's knowledge base rots: files land in the wrong place, names drift,
duplicates pile up, structure erodes. dig is the delegate that does the librarian's whole
job — **find, organize, dedupe, label, version, reconcile** — over one content-addressed
core, safely, even while humans and other agents touch the same library concurrently.

Beyond file management, dig is positioned as the **data/retrieval/memory layer for AI
agents**: organize a KB to *your* mental model, retrieve it fast, remember across sessions,
and plug it into any agent or framework (MCP first, then native SDKs). Bring-your-own
organization model (PARA, GTD, Memory Palace, Zettelkasten, or your own). Your own system
end-to-end — and the data layer others build on.

Design pillars:
- **Open source, runs fully locally.** No cloud account, no telemetry, no required network.
  The store, index, policy engine, dedupe, and merge are all on-device.
- **No LLM required.** The deterministic core does the whole job alone. AI is an *optional*
  layer for fuzzy judgment, off by default.
- **The harness carries the intelligence, not the model.** dig exposes strong deterministic
  CLI tools; a model only makes small bounded judgments — so a small local model suffices.
- **Reversibility is the spine.** Every mutation is journaled and `undo`-able, including
  AI- and auto-applied ones.

## 2. Architecture

**Content-addressed store.** Files hash (BLAKE3) to blobs, stored once. A `manifest` is a
versioned tree mapping paths → blobs. Manifests + a journal live in a per-KB `.dig/`
directory. Identical content → identical blob → dedupe and free "moves" fall out of the
model: a renamed/moved file re-uses its blob and re-indexes/re-embeds nothing.

**Seams (interfaces).** `StorageBackend` (where blobs live), `IndexBackend` (where the
searchable index lives). First-party impls: local filesystem blobs; SQLite FTS5 (pure-Go,
no cgo) for search; an opt-in vector index. Indexes are **derived views** — always rebuilt
from a manifest, never a source of truth.

**Policy → changeset.** Declarative TOML rules compile to `file → {path, name, labels}`.
Running policy over the head manifest produces a **changeset** (a draft manifest + an op
list), never a direct disk mutation. Disk is touched only at commit, atomically, after the
journal entry is written.

**Reconcile loop.** Policy is the desired state; disk is the actual state; drift is the
diff. dig is a reconciler (the Kubernetes-controller pattern for files): `dig drift` reports
misfiled/misnamed/duplicated/unsorted + external human edits; `dig reconcile` converges
one-shot. Human moves are detected via blob identity and treated as intent (pinned), never
overwritten — they escalate instead.

**Concurrency.** Multiple workers operate on isolated work views ({base manifest, draft}).
A changeset state machine (DRAFT→PROPOSED→STAGED→MERGED/CONFLICT/ESCALATED/ABORTED) governs
merges. Commit is a CAS + disjointness check in one serialized transaction; overlapping
changes conflict and the head is left untouched. Compatible ops union; policy precedence
resolves deterministically; unresolved conflicts lock only their subtree while the rest
merges. Race-tested with concurrent workers.

**Watch.** `dig watch` runs continuously — observe edits, reconcile per tick, surface an
escalation queue. Autonomy is earned rule-by-rule: only rules marked `autonomy = "auto"`
apply unattended; everything else proposes.

**AI layer (optional, local, small-model-first).** One OpenAI-compatible client
(`base_url` + `model`) — a local runtime (Ollama / llama.cpp / LM Studio / vLLM) or a
gateway (LiteLLM / OpenRouter). It never touches files directly; it proposes through the
same changeset → state-machine path. Modes: `tools` → `json` → `off`. With `off` or a
localhost endpoint, dig makes zero external network calls.

## 3. Semantic retrieval (shipped)

`dig find` is deterministic FTS by default. A `[retrieval]` policy turns on a **vector
index**: file text is chunked, embedded through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint contract,
and stored in `.dig/vectors.db` as a derived view rebuilt from manifests.

- **Modes:** `dig find --mode fts|vector|hybrid`. Hybrid fuses FTS ∪ vector candidates with
  Reciprocal Rank Fusion (k=60).
- **Blob-keyed embeddings:** moves/renames re-embed nothing; only new content costs.
- **Explicit cache invalidation:** model / prefix / chunking change drops the vector cache
  wholesale (mixing embedding spaces would corrupt ranking).
- **Background indexing:** scans sync the docs view instantly and queue unseen blobs; only a
  small budget embeds inline. `dig embed` drains the backlog with per-file commits
  (interruptible, resumable); `dig watch` drains it per tick. An unreachable endpoint warns
  and never blocks the deterministic spine.
- **Model selection is pure config.** Validated on llama.cpp/CPU: `nomic-embed-text-v1.5`
  (English, with `search_document:`/`search_query:` prefixes), `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (English,
  light), `bge-m3` (multilingual + cross-lingual — a German query finds an English note).

## 4. CLI surface

```
dig init <root>            create a KB at a directory
dig scan                   index files into the content-addressed store (+ FTS, + queue vectors)
dig find <query>           search; --mode fts|vector|hybrid, --json, --limit
dig embed                  drain the semantic-index backlog (resumable)
dig export                 emit a reproducible, manifest-pinned dataset (JSONL); --at, --filter
dig org [--dry-run]        apply organization policy (move/rename/label)
dig dedup [--dry-run]      collapse duplicates per policy
dig drift [--json]         report divergence from policy + external edits
dig reconcile              converge to policy (auto where safe, propose/escalate otherwise)
dig watch [--interval]     run continuously: observe, reconcile, surface escalations
dig work <create|list|abort> / dig merge <name>    isolated work views + merge
dig policy validate        lint the policy, explain rule matches
dig log                    browse change history
dig undo                   revert the last changeset (disk reversed for mutations)
dig --version              build metadata
```

Read commands (`find`, `export`, `drift`, `log`) support `--json` for other harnesses.

## 5. Policy (`.dig/policy.toml`)

```toml
[[rule]]
name  = "invoices"
match = { ext = ["pdf"], content_matches = "invoice" }
into  = "finance/invoices/{year}"
label = ["finance"]
autonomy = "auto"            # "" | "auto" | "propose"

[dedup]
strategy   = "keep-oldest"   # keep-oldest | keep-newest
on_conflict = "escalate"     # never silently delete

[retrieval]
mode     = "hybrid"          # off (default) | hybrid | vector
base_url = "http://127.0.0.1:8092/v1"
model    = "nomic-embed-text-v1.5"
doc_prefix   = "search_document: "
query_prefix = "search_query: "
api_key_env  = "DIG_EMBED_API_KEY"   # env var name; keys never stored in the file
```

Matchers: `ext`, `mime`, `path` glob, `content_matches` regex, size/date. Templates:
`{year} {month} {day} {name} {ext}`. Validation is strict — unknown keys and path-escapes
are rejected loudly. Template targets must stay inside the KB root.

## 6. Measured results (docs/evals.md)

All numbers are retrieval recall through dig's real scan → store → index pipeline, fully
local on CPU with small open embedding models — no reranker, no LLM in the loop.

- **LongMemEval-S, full official 500 questions, 19,829 sessions** (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`):
  hybrid hit@5 **98.0%**, vector 97.8%, FTS 66.8%. The published bar (MemPalace,
  `recall_any@5`) is 96.6% — **beaten**; their architecture-enabled scores (89.4% rooms /
  84.2% compressed) are further back.
- **LoCoMo, 1,536 questions** (`nomic-embed-text-v1.5`): hybrid recall@5 85.3% vs FTS 80.4%;
  hybrid beats FTS on every metric from rank 3 up.
- **BEAM 128K tier, 355 questions** (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`): vector hit@10 64.8% vs FTS 58.3%;
  semantic beats FTS on every metric. (On this turn-level-evidence benchmark vector edges
  hybrid — weak lexical rankings dilute RRF.) Larger tiers in progress.

Honesty note: retrieval recall and LLM-judged QA accuracy are different metrics; recall is
structurally higher. dig reports retrieval metrics because dig is the retrieval/memory
layer — answering belongs to the agent driving it.

## 7. Extensibility

Eight typed seams across four transport tiers (T0 declarative → T1 PATH subcommands → T2
gRPC subprocess → T3 sandboxed WASM): StorageBackend, IndexBackend, event sinks, extractors,
matchers, commands, workflow steps, the model endpoint. The core spine (store semantics,
changeset state machine, undo) is **not** extensible — extensions plug into the edges, never
the spine. See `docs/extensions.md`.

## 8. Project facts

- License: MIT. Module: `github.com/vllnt/dig`. Status: pre-1.0, private, active.
- Build/test gate: `go build ./...` · `go vet ./...` · `go test -race ./...` ·
  `golangci-lint run ./...` · `gofmt -l`. CI enforces all on every PR; `main` is protected.
- Releases: GoReleaser cross-compiles checksummed binaries (linux/darwin/windows ×
  amd64/arm64) on a `vX.Y.Z` tag. Install: `go install github.com/vllnt/dig/cmd/dig@latest`.
- Roadmap (ROADMAP.md): shipped — the reversible file-management lifecycle + semantic
  retrieval. Planned — public release, site launch, eval-harness CI, harness plugins (MCP
  server, portable skill), extensibility, mental-model presets, remote storage, entity
  graph, composable pipeline, agent memory, framework integrations, distribution.
